title: Maximum Ice Cream Bars
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C proposal: the memory extreme. The recommended and fastest-runtime counting-sort proposal is in C++; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The memory objective still follows the mandatory counting-sort technique, but trims the table to the largest initially affordable price. C represents that table as one raw calloc buffer with no vector, object, GC, or interpreter overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Excellent for the recommended fixed-table runtime path, but vector-based capped storage adds a small container baseline and the fixed stack table spends the full R domain.
Selected. One calloc block sized to the affordable domain is the leanest practical implementation of the counting table on LeetCode.
Native and memory-safe, but Vec allocation and checked indexing do not reduce the footprint below the direct C buffer.
Slices are convenient, but the runtime and GC baseline are larger than C for a memory-focused proposal.
Primitive arrays work, but managed-runtime overhead is higher than C's single allocation.
Object-heavy or VM-heavy numeric storage is dominated for minimum memory.

variants.approachJustification: Find the largest initially affordable price, allocate only that many counters, count affordable prices, and greedily consume buckets from cheapest upward.

reasoningSummary: The greedy cheapest-first order is optimal by exchange argument. This memory proposal keeps the required counting-sort technique but shrinks the direct-addressed table to the largest price that can possibly be bought.
